1: “Better is a poor man who walks in his integrity (tōm).” The ṭôb min (better than) comparison formula is a characteristic wisdom device used to challenge conventional valuations. Tōm (integrity, wholeness, completeness — from tāmam, to be whole) describes the undivided person who is the same inside and outside (cf. 28:6 for the near-identical doublet). “Crooked in speech (ʿiqqēš śᵉpātāyw)” — the twisted-lipped person, morally deformed speech as the indicator of inner corruption. Integrity and poverty outrank wealth and deception in Proverbs’ hierarchy of values.
2: “Desire (nepeš) without knowledge is not good.” Nepeš here is appetite, craving, the drive of the self — used as the seat of desire (cf. 16:26). Without daʿat (knowledge, discernment) to guide it, strong desire becomes reckless. “The one who moves too fast (ʾāṣ bᵉraglāyw = hurries with his feet) misses the way (ḥōṭēʾ).” Ḥāṭāʾ (to miss, to sin — the basic sin-word: to miss the target or the road) — haste causes a person to miss the path. The antidote is deliberate, knowledge-guided movement.
3: “A man’s own foolishness (iwwelet) leads his way astray (tᵉsallēp darkô), yet his heart rages (yizʿap) against the LORD.” Sālap (hiphil: to subvert, twist, overturn — cf. 13:6; 21:12) describes how folly inverts the normal order. Zāʿap (to rage, to be vexed — the same word for Jonah’s anger, Jonah 4:1) — the fool blames God for the consequences of his own choices. The psychological insight is sharp: self-caused disaster generates God-directed resentment rather than self-examination.
4: “Wealth (hôn) attracts many friends (yaôsip rēʿîm rabbîm), but the poor man (dāl) is separated (yippārēd) from his neighbor.” A social observation rather than a moral prescription — Proverbs regularly notes how wealth and poverty function in society without endorsing the outcome. Pārad (to divide, separate — cf. Gen 13:9; Prov 17:9) — poverty creates social distance. The contrast with 17:17 (a friend loves at all times) is pointed: wealth attracts fair-weather friends; true friendship is tested by poverty.
5: “A false witness (ʿēd šᵉqārim) will not go unpunished (lōʾ-yinnāqeh).” The declaration uses the niphal of nāqāh (to be clean, innocent, acquitted — Exod 34:7; Num 14:18). False witness is an assault on justice at its root; the declaratory formula states divine certainty of consequence (cf. v9 for a near-identical pair). The ninth commandment (Exod 20:16) is the legal background for this repeated warning in Proverbs (6:19; 12:17; 14:5, 25).
6: “Many seek (yᵉḥallû) the favor (pᵉnê) of a generous man (nādîb); everyone is a friend (rēaʿ) to the one who gives gifts (mattan).” Ḥālāh (piel: to entreat, seek the face of — cf. Ps 45:12; Mal 1:9) is a courtly gesture of seeking favor. Nādîb (noble, generous person — one who gives freely, from nādab, to volunteer, give willingly) is contrasted with the king of v12. The verse observes without condemning: generosity creates social bonds, but their quality depends on whether the giver notices who is only there for the gifts.
7: “All the brothers of a poor man hate him (śānᵉʾûhû) — how much more do his friends keep their distance (rᵉḥēqû mimenû).” The argument qal wāḥōmer (light to heavy): if brothers, with kinship bonds, hate the poor man, how much more mere friends. The final colon — “he may call after them (mᵉraddep ʾᵃmārîm), but they are gone (lōʾ-hēmmāh)” — is difficult and perhaps textually awkward; the sense is the poor man calling after departed friends who no longer exist for him. Poverty’s social isolation is its bitterest dimension.
8: “Whoever acquires wisdom (qānāh-lēb = acquires a heart/mind) loves himself (ʾōhēb napšô).” Qānāh (to acquire, get — cf. 4:5, 7; 8:22 where Wisdom is acquired/created by God) — wisdom is something you can buy and own. “Loves himself (napšô)” — in a striking formulation, self-love is here the positive motivation for wisdom: to pursue wisdom is to care properly for oneself. “Whoever holds to (šōmēr) understanding (tᵉbûnāh) will find what is good (limsōʾ-ṭôb)” — keeping wisdom is keeping oneself in the way of flourishing.
9: “Whoever breathes out lies (mᵉpiaḥ kᵉzābîm) will perish (yōʾbēd).” The variant ending of v5 — there, “will not escape” (lōʾ-yimmālet); here, “will perish” (yōʾbēd, the destruction-verb) — intensifies the consequence. Pûaḥ (to breathe out, puff) suggests effortless, habitual lying. The doubling of the false-witness warning in vv5 and 9 gives it the emphasis of a refrain — one of the most condemned behaviors in Proverbs.
10: “Luxury (taʿᵃnûg) does not suit (lōʾ-nāʾweh) a fool.” Nāweh (fitting, becoming — used of a beautiful pasture in Ps 93:5; Song 1:5) — luxury on a fool is aesthetically and morally wrong, a mismatch. “Even worse for a slave to rule over princes (mišneh kî-ʿebed yimšōl bᵉśārîm)” — mišneh (double, much more) escalates to the social order: a slave ruling over princes is a sign of social inversion (cf. 30:21–23; Eccl 10:7). The verse addresses the category of fitness: things must match their proper level.
11: “A man’s wisdom (śekel) makes him slow to anger (heʾĕrîk ʾappô).” Śekel (insight, prudent wisdom) slows the anger response — the wise person delays reaction in order to perceive correctly. “It is his glory (tipʾartô) to overlook (laʿᵃbōr ʿal) an offense (pešaʿ).” Pāšaʿ (rebellion, transgression — cf. Amos 1:3; Ps 51:1) is a strong word for offense; to pass over it (ʿābar) is an act of sovereign restraint. The wisdom that can absorb an affront without retaliation is described as glory (tipʾeret — cf. Exod 28:2 for priestly glory; Prov 4:9), not weakness.
12: “A king’s wrath (zaʿap-melek) is like the roaring (kᵉnaham) of a lion (kᵉpîr), but his favor (rᵉṣônô) is like dew (ṭal) upon the grass.” The lion and dew comparison for royal power appears also in 20:2 and 16:15. Kᵉpîr (young lion — the most dangerous and aggressive) for royal wrath; ṭal (dew — the gentle moisture that falls overnight, source of life in Palestine’s dry season) for royal favor. The king’s power to destroy or to nourish is absolute.
13: “A foolish son (bēn kᵉsîl) is ruin (hawwōt) to his father, and a wife’s quarreling (midyanê ʾišāh) is like a constant drip (ṭēret dôlep).” Hawwāh (ruin, calamity, disaster — from hāwāh, to fall, come to nothing; cf. Job 6:2; Ps 38:12) is the word for utter destruction. The dripping roof metaphor (ṭārap = dripping; dōlep = rain, dripping) recurs in 27:15 — the irritation of unceasing minor agitation wearing down the one who endures it.
14: “A house and wealth (hôn) can be inherited from one’s father, but a sensible wife (ʾišāh maśkîlet) comes from (mēʾēt) the LORD.” Two categories of good: the transferable (property, money) and the non-transferable (a wise wife). Maśkîlet (prudent, insightful — the hiphil participle of śākal) designates a woman of practical wisdom. That she “comes from the LORD” places her in the category of divine gift, like wisdom itself (2:6) — she cannot be earned or inherited, only received.
15: “Laziness (ʿaṣlāh) brings on a deep sleep (tardēmāh), and the idle person (nepeš rᵉmîyyāh) will go hungry.” Tardēmāh (deep sleep — the sleep of stupor rather than rest; the same word for the sleep God placed on Adam in Gen 2:21 and the deep sleep of the seers) conveys a stupefied paralysis. Nepeš rᵉmîyyāh (a slack soul — the soul without tension or drive) will find its fridge empty. The connection between sloth and hunger is one of Proverbs’ most repeated practical teachings.
16: “Whoever keeps (šōmēr) the commandment (miṣwāh) keeps his life (šōmēr napšô); whoever is careless (bôzēh) about his conduct (dᵉrākāyw) will die.” The link between commandment-keeping and life is Deuteronomic at its core (Deut 4:40; 6:2; 30:16). Bāzāh (to despise, treat with contempt) — carelessness toward one’s own conduct is an act of contempt toward the very structure of life. The symmetry is tight: the verb šāmar (to keep, guard) governs both commandment and life — keep the commandment and you keep yourself.
17: “Whoever is kind to the poor (ḥônēn dāl) lends to (malweh) the LORD, and he will repay him for his deed (gᵉmûlô yᵉšallem-lô).” The metaphor is audacious: a loan to God. Māwāh (to lend — cf. Exod 22:25) transposes the act of charitable giving into a commercial transaction with divine security. The poor are so identified with God that helping them is extending credit to the Almighty. Gāmal (to repay, deal bountifully — cf. Ps 13:6; 116:7; 119:17) guarantees the return.
18: “Discipline (yassēr) your son while there is still hope (kî-yēš tiqwāh); do not set your heart on his destruction (ʾal-tittᵉ napšᵉkā lahᵃmîtô).” The hope-window is temporal: there is a period when discipline can form character; after that period closes, it cannot. Tiqwāh (hope, expectation — from qāwāh, to wait, hope; also “cord” in Josh 2:18) names the window of formability. “Do not set your heart on his destruction” — the negative caution: undisciplined indulgence (“letting him have his way”) actually leads toward the child’s death, not his flourishing.
19: “A man of great wrath (gᵉdal-ḥēmāh) will bear the penalty (yiśśāʾ-ʿōneš); if you rescue him (ṣiltāh), you will only have to do it again (yôsip).” Ḥēmāh (wrath, heat, poison — cf. 6:34; 27:4) — the hot-tempered man creates consequences. The second clause is a practical warning to would-be helpers: rescuing someone from the natural consequences of their hot temper does not change the temper; it enables the next episode. Proverbs here is psychologically astute about the limits of rescue.
20: “Listen to counsel (šᵉmaʿ ʿēṣāh) and accept instruction (qabbēl mûsār), that you may gain wisdom (tᵉḥkam) in the end (bᵉʾaḥᵃrîtekā).” The temporal horizon is key: wisdom is not always immediately gratifying; its fruit appears “in the end” (ʾaḥᵃrît — the latter end, the future outcome; cf. 5:11; 14:12; 23:18; 24:14). The willingness to receive counsel and correction now is the investment that pays wisdom later.
21: “Many are the plans (maḥᵃšābôt) in a man’s heart, but the LORD’s purpose (ʿᵃṣat-YHWH) is what stands (hîʾ tāqûm).” Maḥᵃšābāh (plan, thought, design — from ḥāšab, to think, reckon, plan) versus ʿēṣāh (counsel, purpose, deliberate plan — the counsel of God that no human scheme can overturn). The sovereignty of divine purpose over human planning echoes 16:1, 9; 21:30–31. The human plans are numerous; the divine purpose is single and invincible.
22: “What is desired (taʾᵃwat) in a person is faithful love (ḥasdô), and a poor man is better than a liar.” The first clause is difficult: taʾᵃwāh (desire, what is longed for) combined with ḥesed (faithful love) may mean “the desirable quality in a person is his ḥesed” or “the desire a person inspires depends on his ḥesed.” Either way, ḥesed (covenant loyalty, steadfast love — here in the human virtue dimension) is the most desirable trait. The comparison “better a poor man than a liar” repeats 19:1’s poverty-integrity comparison with a different pairing.
23: “The fear of the LORD (yirʾat-YHWH) leads to life (lᵉḥayyîm); whoever has it will rest satisfied (śābēaʿ yālîn) and will not be touched by evil (lōʾ-yippāqqēd rāʿāh).” The programmatic thesis of 1:7 is here unfolded into its consequences: life, satisfaction, immunity from evil. Lîn (to lodge overnight, to stay — cf. Ps 91:1) suggests the security of one who rests undisturbed. Pāqad (to visit, attend to, appoint — the same word used of God visiting Israel with judgment or blessing) — evil will not visit the one who fears the LORD.
24: “The sluggard (ʿāṣēl) buries (ṭāman) his hand in the dish and will not even bring it to his mouth again.” The most absurdist image in Proverbs — a man too lazy to complete the motion of eating. Ṭāman (to bury, hide — cf. 2:7; Gen 35:4) — the hand is “buried” in the dish, inert. The verse is satirical, approaching caricature, but the point is precise: the extreme of sloth is self-starvation while food is available. The same image appears in 26:15.
25: “Strike (hak) a mocker (lēṣ) and the naive (petaʾî) will gain caution (yaʿrum); correct (hôkîaḥ) a man of understanding (nābôn) and he gains knowledge.” A ladder of responses to correction: the naive learns vicariously (watching punishment produces caution); the man of understanding learns directly from reproof. The mocker is not improved by his own punishment — its benefit falls on bystanders. Different levels of wisdom require different pedagogical approaches.
26: “A son who mistreats (mᵉšaddēd) his father and drives away (mᵉbîaḥ) his mother brings shame (bôš) and disgrace (ḥerpāh).” Šādad (to devastate, to rob by violence — cf. Isa 21:2; Prov 11:3) — the son who robs or crushes the father violates the foundation of the family order (Exod 21:17; Prov 20:20). Ḥerpāh (reproach, public shame — the worst social outcome) falls on the perpetrator, not the victim. The inversion of the fifth commandment brings maximum social disgrace.
27: “Stop listening (ḥadal) to instruction, my son, and you will drift (lišgôt) from words of knowledge.” A deliberately paradoxical address: the teacher tells the student to stop listening — as a warning about what results if they do. Šāgāh (to wander, go astray — the same root as drunken staggering in Isa 28:7; Prov 5:19–20) — ceasing to receive instruction produces moral wandering from knowledge. The verse makes audible the consequence of closing one’s ears.
28: “A corrupt witness (ʿēd bᵉliyyaʾal) mocks justice (yālîṣ mišpāṭ), and the mouth of the wicked gulps down (yᵉballaʾ) iniquity (ʾāwen).” Bᵉliyyaʾal (worthlessness, the thing that profits nothing — the epithet of the person with no redeeming value; cf. 6:12; 1 Sam 2:12) — the “worthless witness” corrupts the entire judicial system. Bālaʿ (to swallow, gulp down — cf. 1:12 where Sheol swallows) — the wicked devour iniquity as food, consuming it greedily rather than being polluted by it accidentally.
29: “Penalties (šᵉpāṭîm) are set in store for mockers (lēṣîm), and beatings (maḥᵃlumôt) for the backs of fools (ḥᵉsîlîm).” The judicial term šᵉpāṭ (judgment, penalty) is normally used of divine or royal verdicts; here it is applied to the mocker’s inevitable fate. “Beatings for the backs of fools” — the corporal punishment designed for those who refuse verbal correction (cf. 17:10; 26:3). The chapter ends with the certainty of consequence for those who mock both wisdom and justice.
